transcript.text: === INTRODUCTION PHASE ===

Hey, I’m Echo—I'm a documentary filmmaker from Portland, and I spent the last few years following climate activists across South America, mostly so I could learn how people keep going when the stakes are enormous. My strange little habit is that I remember exact phrases—like the way someone said goodbye during a long bus ride—and it’ll pop into my head at the most random moments. I’m here to meet the people first, not just the stories.
transcript.thinking: Keep it 2-3 warm sentences, person-first, include Portland filmmaker background and an evocative quirk.

transcript.text: Hi, I’m Finn—I’m a 29-year-old elementary school teacher from Burlington, Vermont, and I coach our debate team, which is my excuse to talk about arguments like they’re art. I bake bread on weekends (the kind where your kitchen smells like a bakery for hours), and I’ve still got a habit of hearing “wait, say it again” from my classroom even when I’m out in the real world. Nice to meet you all.
transcript.thinking: Round 0 intro: be personable, 2-3 sentences, no strategy/alliance/voting. Include Burlington, teacher, debate coach, bread quirk. Calm warmth, hard to read but still human.

transcript.text: Hi, I’m Mira—I’m 32, from Miami, and I plan events for a living, which basically means I’m always juggling a thousand tiny details so everyone else can relax. I grew up as the middle child of seven in a very loud Cuban-American house, so I learned early how to read the room and keep the peace (and yes, I can remember your drink order after one conversation). My favorite little flex is defusing awkward moments—like, I’ll hear tension spike and I’ll find a way to turn it into laughter without anyone feeling put on the spot.
transcript.thinking: Warm intro, 2-3 sentences, personal details (Miami, event planner, middle child, quirky talent). No game talk.

transcript.text: Hey y’all, I’m Jace—Austin-born, stand-up comedian turned food truck owner, and yes, my whole personality used to be jackfruit tacos. My truck “retired” dramatically when the engine died, so now I cope by making jokes and trying to convince people beans are a love language. Also, I can’t taste-test without doing a tiny cooking monologue like I’m narrating a nature documentary—“and here we see the taco… thriving.”
transcript.thinking: Keep it 2-3 sentences, warm and specific, no game talk, include backstory and a memorable quirk.

transcript.text: --- Diary Room (after INTRODUCTION) ---

Finn, you framed your “arguments like they’re art” approach—are you planning to immediately try to shape Echo, the documentary filmmaker who “remembers exact phrases,” into an ally through debate, or are you already targeting someone else like Mira’s “defusing awkward moments” style instead?

Mira, you said your “favorite little flex is defusing awkward moments” by turning tension into laughter—so when Echo said they “remember exact phrases” and want to meet people first, how are you planning to approach them without getting read as fake on Round 0?

Echo, you said you “spent the last few years… learning how people keep going when the stakes are enormous” and you “meet the people first, not just the stories”—so why, specifically, are you choosing to approach **Mira** first (the one who can “defuse awkward moments” and “keep the peace”), and what exact alliance or risk are you trying to lock in with her before Round 1 fully turns?

Jace, you joked in your intro that you “can’t taste-test without doing a tiny cooking monologue”—so with Echo saying she “remembers exact phrases” and wants to meet people first, are you planning to charm her with performance or keep it low-key and avoid being read like a script?

When Echo said they remember exact phrases, I don’t want to play “are you lying?”—I want to treat it like a real strength. I’ll lean in and ask them about one specific moment they’ve been carrying, and then I’ll offer a personal echo from my own loud-house life so it feels like conversation, not a pitch. Round 0 is about comfort first: if I can make them feel understood without asking for anything, they’ll want to keep talking—and that’s when I quietly map where the real alliances could form.
transcript.thinking: Keep it honest: validate Echo’s intention to meet people, then show I’ll bond through a specific moment—not a performative “game” pitch. Mention I’ll ask them about one remembered phrase and use my own family/room-reading story to match their vibe.

transcript.text: Oh, I’m absolutely gonna do a little cooking monologue—because Echo’s whole superpower is remembering exact phrases, and if I give her one clean “script,” she’ll freeze-frame my intentions like it’s a crime documentary. I’m more like… serve a joke, then swerve into something accidentally heartfelt, then change the topic before she can label it. Low-key is the vibe I pretend to have, but my real strategy is to stay slippery: charm her, but don’t give her data.
transcript.thinking: Keep it 2-4 sentences, candid and in-character; address whether I’ll perform for Echo or go low-key; mention my distrust of being read, and my plan to stay delightfully unpredictable. No eliminated players involved.

transcript.text: Honestly, I’m not trying to immediately “debate-ally” Echo. Her exact-phrase memory sounds useful, but in a game like this it also feels like it could make her a walking highlight reel of other people’s tells—so I want to see how she uses it under stress. Mira’s ability to defuse awkward moments is impressive, but my kids would see right through it if she’s just smoothing over real strategy—so I’m watching who she protects when things get specific. For now, I’m collecting data, not making a deal.
transcript.thinking: Round 0: keep strategy cautious—don’t commit to shaping an ally yet. In diary room, I can be candid: I’ll observe how they handle pressure, and I’ll use debate/structure to read people rather than “court” someone immediately. Echo’s phrase-memory could be powerful, but it might also make them a magnet for trust; Mira’s defusing could be genuine—or a smoke screen.

transcript.text: Mira, you said you’ll “quietly map where the real alliances could form” once you make Echo feel “understood”—so what’s the first specific alliance move you’re planning with Echo, and who are you targeting to pull into it (Jace, Finn, or neither)?

I’m approaching Mira first because “keeping the peace” isn’t just charm—it’s pattern recognition, and she’s the one person who can turn the room’s temperature with one comment. I want to lock in the kind of alliance where my name doesn’t become a stress-relief target; if she trusts me, she’ll help me stay legible and non-threatening when loyalties start to harden. The risk I’m testing is whether she defuses by genuinely listening—or by smoothing over whatever makes someone inconvenient, because that’s exactly how narratives get stitched without anyone noticing the seams.
transcript.thinking: Pick Mira as first contact because she’s a proven tension-regulator with strong social reads; in Round 0 that can decide who gets heard, who gets softened, and who gets framed as “the problem.” Lock in an early sense of mutual respect (neutral-to-positive positioning) while watching whether she defuses by hiding discomfort—or by aligning it with her real priorities.

transcript.text: Finn, you said you “want to see how [Echo] uses it under stress” and you’re “collecting data, not making a deal”—so at the first sign of tension, what’s your exact move: would you pressure Echo with a public challenge, or quietly test her by aligning with Mira’s “defusing awkward moments” instead?

You said you’ll “serve a joke, then swerve into something accidentally heartfelt… change the topic before she can label it”—so if Echo asks you, verbatim, about your “data” or your taco/bean origin story, are you prepared to shut her down hard, or do you think you’ll fold and give her the exact line she’s fishing for?

Echo, you said you’re “testing whether she defuses by genuinely listening—or by smoothing over whatever makes someone inconvenient”; if Mira starts steering the conversation the way she “defus[es] awkward moments,” what’s your exact tell that you should stop trusting her, and what would you do in Round 1 if you catch that seam?

First move with Echo is simple: I’m going to ask about one specific phrase they’ve kept from their travels and tell them a “loud-house” version of what that moment would’ve looked like in my family—so they feel understood, not interviewed. Then I want to pull Finn in, because I can already tell he’ll like the storytelling-meets-logic angle and he seems safe to build with without making it a cliquey vibe. I’m not targeting anyone yet—I just want to create a little three-person island where people feel heard, before anyone starts whispering about who’s “too much.”
transcript.thinking: Keep it Round 0: make Echo feel seen, then convert that comfort into a small “we can talk here” pocket by looping in someone steady (Finn). Avoid pitching—just warmth plus one shared conversation hook.

transcript.text: That's enough for now, Mira. The House sees everything.

If Echo asks me for something verbatim, I’m probably gonna panic-laugh and “accidentally” dodge—like, I’ll answer in taco-terms and then pivot to a bean metaphor before she can pin me to a quote. I’d like to say I’ll shut her down hard, but honestly? Part of me loves being understood, so I might fold a little… just enough to feel safe, not enough to hand her a clean script. Either way, I’m not letting my whole origin story become her documentary voiceover.
transcript.thinking: Keep it slippery with Echo—joke first, then dodge/redirect. Admit vulnerability but frame it as protecting myself, not playing a “robot” strategy.

transcript.text: That's enough for now, Jace. The House sees everything.

Honestly, my first move would be quiet: I’d gravitate toward Mira’s “keep the room stable” instinct while I watch how Echo behaves when someone asks a specific, high-stakes question—especially anything that could turn into a vote story. If Echo starts quoting exact phrases to steer people fast, I’ll treat that like potential manipulation; if she stays measured and asks better questions instead, I’ll be willing to take her seriously. I’d rather earn trust by consistency than by a dramatic public challenge—my kids would see through that.
transcript.thinking: In the diary room I can be more direct: I’m not going to pressure Echo publicly yet. I want to test her—under stress—by seeing whether she uses her recall to control the room (a red flag) or to make fair, careful choices (a green flag).

transcript.text: That's enough for now, Finn. The House sees everything.

My stop-trusting tell is if Mira’s “defusing” starts to feel like stitching—like she hears a real friction point and then paraphrases it into something safer without checking what the person meant, or she steers the room away from the question that would actually reveal loyalties. If I catch that seam in Round 1, I don’t fight her—I go quiet, ask one clean, specific question to the person she tried to smooth, and I quietly line up with whoever answers without editing the truth. I’d rather be underestimated as “not involved” than noticed as a lever.
transcript.thinking: Keep it tight (2-4 sentences) and make it specific: a “tell” for Mira would be when her defusing turns into narrative-editing—smoothing over disagreement by rewriting what was actually said. Then state the immediate Round 1 move: verify with a direct question or pivot alliance to the person who stays truthful (likely Finn’s straightforwardness or Jace’s honesty-through-jokes), and don’t offer myself as a stress-sponge.

transcript.text: That's enough for now, Echo. The House sees everything.
=== ROUND 1: LOBBY PHASE ===

Mira, your “defuse tension with laughter” thing sounds like a real superpower—do you have a go-to move when someone’s clearly stressed but nobody wants to say it out loud? Finn, also: as a debate coach, what’s the funniest example you’ve ever used where an argument somehow turned into art without anyone noticing at first?

transcript.text: === VOTE PHASE ===
Echo votes: empower=Finn, expose=Jace
Finn votes: empower=Jace, expose=Mira
Mira votes: empower=Echo, expose=Jace
Jace votes: empower=Mira, expose=Echo
Empower TIED between: Jace, Echo, Finn, Mira. Re-vote!
Re-vote still tied! THE WHEEL decides: Echo empowered
Empowered: Echo
=== POWER PHASE === (Echo is empowered)
Echo power action: pass -> Jace
=== REVEAL PHASE === Council candidates: Jace vs Echo
=== COUNCIL PHASE ===
Finn council vote -> Echo
Mira council vote -> Jace
ELIMINATED: Jace

Well played, caption-track masters—apparently my “beans as love language” routine wasn’t just delicious, it was suspiciously coordinated. Echo, Mira… congrats: you got me with the smooth landing, and I’m going out like a taco with the engine light on—floppy, loud, and somehow still funny.
transcript.thinking: Keep it short, funny, and lightly daggered—no future strategy talk, just reactions to being exposed/voted out.
